narrative:
On July 9, 2026, the Karnataka High Court recorded an overall hearing rate of 44.3%, with 1,125 cases heard out of 2,541 scheduled. This reflects a notable increase in operational throughput compared to the 37.5% heard on July 8 and 39.2% on July 7. The court maintained parity in processing, with a minimal bias gap of 0.3% between government-related (44.5%) and commoner-related (44.2%) matters.

Judge-wise performance showed significant variance. Justice M. Nagaprasanna (Hall 14) and Justice Shivashankar Amarannavar (Hall 20) demonstrated high productivity, achieving individual session clearance rates of 78.0% and 75.0%, respectively. Conversely, some benches, such as the Chief Justice & K.S. Hemalekha (Hall 1) and B.M. Shyam Prasad (Hall 6), experienced sessions with minimal movement, recording hearing rates as low as 0% and 0.9% in specific portfolios, despite high throughput in other dedicated lists.

Analysis of hearing stages highlights a focus on procedural and entry-level matters. Admission (49.6%) and Preliminary Hearing (46.9%) stages maintained healthy clearance rates, while Final Hearings continued to face challenges with a 23.0% clearance rate, though this represents a slight improvement over the 14.7% observed on July 8.

Case type distribution was dominated by Writ Petitions (WP), accounting for 1,012 scheduled cases with a 40.8% clearance rate. Criminal Petitions (CRL.P) and Miscellaneous First Appeals (MFA) showed higher relative fluidity, with clearance rates of 51.6% and 50.0%, respectively. While WP clearance has remained relatively stable (36.9%–42.1%) over the past three days, Criminal Petitions saw a distinct recovery, rising from 22.9% yesterday to 51.6% today, indicating a significant shift in judicial attention toward criminal matters.
